elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we demonstrate how a class of advanced matrix concentration inequalities, introduced in \cite{brailovskaya2024universality , can be used to eliminate the dimensional factor in the convergence rate of matrix completion.}
elements.sent-2411-002: This dimensional factor represents a significant gap between the upper bound and the minimax lower bound, especially in high dimension.
elements.sent-2411-003: Through a more precise spectral norm analysis, we remove the dimensional factors for five different estimators in various settings, thereby establishing their minimax rate optimality.
elements.sent-2411-004: Matrix recovery from a small subset of entries, often referred to as matrix completion, is a classical problem in high-dimensional statistics with wide-ranging applications across various fields.
elements.sent-2411-005: Numerous frameworks and methods have been developed for matrix completion, with a fundamental understanding that a large matrix cannot be recovered unless it exhibits certain low-rank properties.
elements.sent-2411-006: As a convex relaxation of rank penalization, nuclear norm penalized trace regression has become a popular approach and corresponding theoretical research has been fruitful.
elements.sent-2411-008: In particular, many studies focus on the convergence rate of the nuclear norm penalization methods \citep{koltchinskii2011nuclear, negahban2012restricted, klopp2014noisy, yu2024low .}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, the minimax lower bound, obtained in \cite{koltchinskii2011nuclear and \cite{negahban2012restricted , does not include a logarithmic dimension factor that always appears in the upper bound of the convergence rate.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: This discrepancy between the two bounds becomes more pronounced in high-dimensional settings, highlighting a potential gap in the existing theoretical understanding.
elements.sent-2411-011: Due to this issue, previous works have had to qualify their results by stating ``our estimator is minimax optimal, up to a logarithmic factor".
elements.sent-2411-012: In this paper, we use a class of sharp matrix concentration inequalities to remove the logarithmic dimension factor.
elements.sent-2411-013: After our work, the qualification will no longer be necessary.
elements.sent-2411-014: Let $A_0\in \Rb^{m_1\times m_2}$ be the unknown matrix we aim to recover.
elements.sent-2411-015: We observe independent samples $\{(X_i, Y_i)\}_{i=1 ^{\nu}$ from the model \begin{equation}\label{model:mc} Y_i=\la X_i, A_0\ra +\xi_i, \quad i=1,\ldots, \nu,\end{equation} where $X_i\in \mathbb{R ^{m_1\times m_2}$, $\langle X_i, A_0\rangle=\text{tr (X_i^TA_0)$, and $\text{tr (B)$ denotes the trace of matrix $B$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The noise variables $\xi_i$ are independent and identically distributed.
elements.sent-2411-017: The sampling matrices $X_i\in\mathbb{R ^{m_1\times m_2}$ take values from $\{ e_l(m_1)e_k^T(m_2), 1\leq l\leq m_1, 1\leq k\leq m_2\}$, where $e_j(m_1),e_k(m_2)$ denote standard basis vectors in $\mathbb{R ^{m_1 , \mathbb{R ^{m_2}$, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: This implies that each $X_i$ only has one non-zero entry, indicating the location of the sampled entry.
elements.sent-2411-019: The distribution of the noise variables $\xi_i$ will be specified in different settings.
elements.sent-2411-020: The sampling distribution of $X_i$ is denoted by \begin{equation}P_{jk}=P(X_i=e_j(m_1)e_k^T(m_2)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-021: Additionally, $X_i$ and $\xi_i$ are assumed to be independent.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper, we consider that $\text{rank (A_0)\leq r$ or $A_0$ is the sum of a low rank matrix and a sparse matrix, and $\|A_0\|_{\infty}\leq a$ where $\|A\|_{\infty =\max_{1\leq i\leq m_1, 1\leq j\leq m_2 |a_{ij |$ is the entrywise maximum norm of $A=(a_{ij )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The total sample size $\nu=n {\rm ~or~ N$ depending on the settings.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The matrix completion problem is often addressed by a penalized regression.
elements.sent-2411-025: Assuming $A_0$ is of low rank, a natural choice for the penalty is the rank of a matrix.
elements.sent-2411-026: However, the rank penalty is non-convex and makes the corresponding program difficult to solve.
elements.sent-2411-027: The nuclear norm $\|\cdot\|_1$, namely the sum of all the singular values of a matrix, is usually used as a convex relaxation for the rank.
elements.sent-2411-028: There are many classical works on nuclear norm penalization methods under different assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-029: Assuming the noise has exponential decay, \cite{koltchinskii2011nuclear consider the case that the sampling distribution is known; \cite{negahban2012restricted study the situation where the sampling distribution is unknown and the row indices and the column indices of the observations are sampled independently; \cite{klopp2014noisy explores the problem for general sampling distributions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Consider the classical nuclear norm penalized least squares estimator \begin{equation}\hat{A}=\argmin_{\|A\|_\infty\leq a} \left\{ \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i-\langle X_i,A\rangle)^2+\lambda \|A\|_1 \right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-031: Assuming $\text{rank (A_0)\leq r$, $X_i$ has a general non-uniform distribution and the noise has exponential decay, \cite{klopp2014noisy obtains the following bound that holds with high probability: \begin{equation}\dfrac{\|\hat A-A_0\|_F^2}{m_1m_2} \apprle \log (m_1+m_2) \dfrac{r\max(m_1,m_2)}{n} ,\end{equation} where $\apprle$ means that the inequality holds up to some numeric constant, and $\|A\|_F=\sqrt{\sum_{i=1 ^{m_1}\sum_{j=1 ^{m_2 a^2_{ij }$ is the Frobenius norm of $A=(a_{ij )$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, the minimax lower bound, as established in \cite{koltchinskii2011nuclear , does not involve the term $\log (m_1+m_2)$ and is expressed as \begin{equation}\inf_{\hat{A}}\sup_{\mathcal{A}} P\left( \dfrac{\|\hat A-A_0\|_F^2}{m_1m_2} \apprge \dfrac{r \max(m_1,m_2)}{n}\right)\geq C,\end{equation} where $C\in (0,1)$ is a constant, and the infimum and supremum are taken over all the possible estimators and the parameter space $\{A_0\in \mathbb{R ^{m_1\times m_2 : \text{rank (A_0)\leq r, \|A_0\|_{\infty}\leq a\}$ respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The logarithmic dimension factor $\log (m_1+m_2)$, as the gap between the upper and lower bounds, exists in other matrix completion settings as well.
elements.sent-2411-034: See, for instance, \cite{klopp2017robust for corrupted matrix completion, \cite{yu2024low for matrix completion with heavy tailed noise.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Recently, \cite{bandeira2023matrix and \cite{brailovskaya2024universality introduce new matrix concentration inequalities, which establish sharper bounds for certain important random matrices.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: We showcase how the new bounds can be leveraged in the matrix completion problem to drop the logarithmic dimension factor.
elements.sent-2411-037: Specifically, We consider five estimators across three different settings as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-038: First setting: the unknown matrix is of low rank and the noise is sub-Gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-039: This is the most classical setting.
elements.sent-2411-040: Two estimators are studied in \cite{klopp2014noisy , one for the case of known variance and another for the case of unknown variance.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Second setting: the unknown matrix is of low rank and the noise is heavy tailed.
elements.sent-2411-042: The estimator in \cite{yu2024low is designed for the case that the noise is only assumed to have finite second moment.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Third setting: the unknown matrix is corrupted and the noise is sub-Gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-044: Two estimators are studied under different corruption models in \cite{klopp2017robust : entrywise corruption and columnwise corruption.}
elements.sent-2411-045: All the previous results on the convergence rates of the above five estimators involve the logarithmic dimension factor.
elements.sent-2411-046: We remove the dimensional factor for all the five estimators, thereby proving their minimax rate optimality.

elements.sent-2411-187: In this paper, we considered three popular matrix completion settings: (1) the unknown matrix is of low rank and the noise is sub-Gaussian; (2) the unknown matrix is of low rank and the noise is heavy tailed; (3) the unknown matrix is a low rank matrix corrupted by a sparse matrix in the entrywise or columnwise sense, and the noise is sub-Gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-188: We revisited five existing estimators in these settings, and developed sharper upper bounds to establish their minimax rate optimality.
elements.sent-2411-189: The key technical contribution lies in employing powerful matrix concentration inequalities introduced in \cite{brailovskaya2024universality to successfully remove the dimension factor $\log d$ that has remained in the upper bounds prior to our work.}
elements.sent-2411-190: The $\log d$ factor problem not only exists for the fives estimators studied in this paper, but is ubiquitous throughout the literature.
elements.sent-2411-191: We believe that many other existing results on matrix completion can be possibly improved by applying these new advanced inequalities.

elements.sent-2411-533: In this paper, we considered three common matrix completion settings: (1) the unknown matrix is of low rank and the noise is heavy tailed; (2) the unknown matrix is of low rank and the noise is sub-Gaussian with known variance; (3) the unknown matrix is of low rank and the noise is sub-Gaussian with unknown variance.
elements.sent-2411-534: We revisited three popular estimators in these settings and developed sharper upper bounds to establish their minimax rate optimality.

elements.sent-2411-712: In a cleaner form, we present the concentration inequality \begin{equation}\label{inequality: max alpha} \Pb\left(\sup_{i\in\{1,\cdots,n\}}|\xi_i|\geq C_{1,\alpha} \sigma(\log n)^{1/\alpha}+ C_{2,\alpha} \sigma t^{1/\alpha}\right) \leq 2\exp(-t)\end{equation} As a consequence, if $ \{\xi_i\}_{i=1 ^n $ is a sequence of sub-exponential random variables satisfying $ \Vert \xi_i\Vert_{\psi_2}\leq \sigma $, there exist constants $ C_1, C_2 $ such that \begin{equation}\label{inequality: max subgaussian } \Pb\left(\sup_{i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}} |\xi_i|\geq C_1 \sigma\sqrt{\log n}+C_2\sigma\sqrt{t}\right)\leq 2\exp(-t).\end{equation}.}
